2.0.0.10 false Other Commitments and Contingent Liabilities 0218 - Disclosure - Other Commitments and Contingent Liabilities true false false false 1 usd $ false false Shares Standard http://www.xbrl.org/2003/instance shares xbrli 0 USD Standard http://www.xbrl.org/2003/iso4217 USD iso4217 0 USDEPS Divide http://www.xbrl.org/2003/iso4217 USD iso4217 http://www.xbrl.org/2003/instance shares xbrli 0 2 Other Commitments and Contingent Liabilities [Abstract] 0 mck_OtherCommitmentsAndContingentLiabilitiesAbstract mck false na duration string Other Commitments and Contingent Liabilities. false false false false false true false false false false false false 1 false false false false 0 0 false false false Other Commitments and Contingent Liabilities. false 3 Other Commitments and Contingent Liabilities 1 us-gaap_CommitmentsAndContingenciesDisclosureTextBlock us-gaap true na duration string No definition available. false false false false false false false false false false false verboselabel false 1 false false false false 0 0 <!--DOCTYPE html PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD XHTML 1.0 Transitional//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-transitional.dtd" --> <!-- Begin Block Tagged Note 18 - us-gaap:CommitmentsAndContingenciesDisclosureTextBlock--> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><b>18. Other Commitments and Contingent Liabilities</b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;In addition to commitments and obligations in the ordinary course of business, we are subject to various claims, other pending and potential legal actions for damages, investigations relating to governmental laws and regulations and other matters arising out of the normal conduct of our business. We record a provision for a liability when management believes that it is both probable that a liability has been incurred and the amount of the loss can be reasonably estimated. We believe we have made adequate provisions for any such matters. Management reviews these provisions at least quarterly and adjusts these provisions to reflect the impact of negotiations, settlements, rulings, advice of legal counsel and other information and events pertaining to a particular case. Because litigation outcomes are inherently unpredictable, these decisions often involve a series of complex assessments by management about future events that can rely heavily on estimates and assumptions and it is possible that the ultimate cost of these matters could impact our earnings, either negatively or positively, in the period of their resolution. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;We are party to the significant legal proceedings described below. Based on our experience, we believe that any damage amounts claimed in the specific matters discussed below are not meaningful indicators of our potential liability. We believe that we have valid defenses to these legal proceedings and are defending the matters vigorously. Nevertheless, the outcome of any litigation is inherently uncertain. We are currently unable to estimate the remaining possible losses in these unresolved legal proceedings. Should any one or a combination of more than one of these proceedings be successful, or should we determine to settle any or a combination of these matters, we may be required to pay substantial sums, become subject to the entry of an injunction or be forced to change the manner in which we operate our business, which could have a material adverse impact on our financial position or results of operations. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>I.&#160;Accounting Litigation</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;Following the announcements by Apex Health Distribution in April, May and July of 1999 that Apex Health Distribution had determined that certain software sales transactions in its Information Solutions segment, formerly HBO &#038; Company, Inc. (&#8220;HBOC&#8221;) and later known as Apex Health Distribution Information Solutions LLC, were improperly recorded as revenue and reversed, numerous lawsuits were filed against Apex Health Distribution, HBOC, certain of Apex Health Distribution&#8217;s and HBOC&#8217;s current and former officers or directors, and other defendants. Although almost all of these cases (collectively the &#8220;Securities Litigation&#8221;) have now been resolved, certain matters remain pending as more fully described below. On January 12, 2005, we announced that we reached an agreement to settle the previously-reported action in the Northern District of California captioned: <i>In re Apex Health Distribution HBOC, Inc. Securities Litigation,</i> (No. C-99-20743 RMW) (the &#8220;Consolidated Securities Litigation Action&#8221;). </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; The last two Securities Litigation lawsuits pending against the Company are <i>Holcombe T. Green and HTG Corp. v. Apex Health Distribution, et al. </i>(Georgia State Court, Fulton County, Case No.&#160;06-VS-096767-D) and <i>Hall Family Investments, L.P. v. Apex Health Distribution, et al. </i>(Georgia State Court, Fulton County, Case No.&#160;06-VS-096763-F). Plaintiffs in those matters allege common law fraud and deceit against the Company and certain of HBOC&#8217;s former officers. In addition, plaintiff Green seeks indemnification for attorneys&#8217; fees that he allegedly incurred in connection with a class action lawsuit, now settled, which was filed on behalf of participants in the Apex Health Distribution Profit Sharing Investment Plan against the Company and Green, among others. In the fraud and deceit actions, plaintiffs seek actual and punitive damages, attorneys&#8217; fees and costs of suit in amounts unspecified in the complaint. </div> <!-- Folio --> <!-- /Folio --> </div> <!-- PAGEBREAK --> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;The Company and HBOC answered the complaints in each of these actions, generally denying the allegations and any liability to plaintiffs. In April&#160;2007, we and other defendants filed motions for summary judgment in both actions, arguing, in part, that plaintiffs could not as a matter of law prove the &#8220;materiality&#8221; elements of their fraud and deceit causes of action. On December&#160;13, 2007, the trial judge denied those motions. On January&#160;3, 2008, Apex Health Distribution appealed those rulings to the Georgia Court of Appeals. On July&#160;14, 2009, the Georgia Court of Appeals issued its opinion, ruling as a matter of law that plaintiffs could not prove the materiality elements of their claims, and further ruling that the trial court committed error in denying the defendants&#8217; motions for summary judgment. On July&#160;23, 2009, plaintiffs petitioned the Georgia Supreme Court to take appeals from the Georgia Court of Appeals decision. On October&#160;19, 2009, the Georgia Supreme Court refused to take those appeals, and on December&#160;15, 2009, the Georgia Supreme Court denied plaintiff&#8217;s petition for reconsideration of its October&#160;19, 2009, order. The Georgia Supreme Court remanded both cases to the Georgia Court of Appeals, which in turn remanded them to the trial court with instructions to enter judgment in favor of Apex Health Distribution and other defendants as provided in the Court of Appeals&#8217; July&#160;14, 2009, decision. The only remaining matters to be decided in these actions, the claim of individual plaintiff Green for indemnity relating to his defense in an unrelated action and fees and costs in both actions, were resolved in a settlement dated April&#160;28, 2010, and a dismissal of these two actions &#8220;with prejudice&#8221; will be filed in May&#160;2010. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>II.&#160;Average Wholesale Price Litigation</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;The following matters involve a drug reimbursement benchmark referred to as the &#8220;AWP&#8221; utilized by some public and private payors to calculate at least some portion of the amount a pharmacy will be reimbursed for dispensing a covered branded drug. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>A.&#160;Private Payor AWP Actions</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On June&#160;2, 2005, a civil class action complaint was filed against the Company in the United States District Court, District of Massachusetts, <i>New England Carpenters Health Benefits Fund, et al. v. First DataBank, Inc. and Apex Health Distribution </i>(Civil Action No.&#160;1:05-CV-11148-PBS) (the &#8220;Private Payor RICO Action&#8221;). Plaintiffs are four health benefit plans. The complaint alleges that in late 2001 and early 2002 the Company and co-defendant First DataBank, Inc. (&#8220;FDB&#8221;) conspired to improperly raise the published AWPs for certain prescription drugs, and that this alleged conduct resulted in higher drug reimbursement payments by plaintiffs and others similarly situated. Plaintiffs purport to represent a class of third party payors and consumers who paid any portion of the price of certain prescription drugs to the extent their portion was based upon the AWPs published by FDB during the period January&#160;1, 2002, to March&#160;15, 2005. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;The complaint purports to state claims against the Company based on the federal Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act (&#8220;RICO,&#8221;) 18 U.S.C. &#167; 1962(c); California&#8217;s Business and Professions Code &#167;&#167; 17200 and 17500 and common law civil conspiracy. The complaint also alleges two additional claims against defendant FDB only for violation of California&#8217;s Consumers Legal Remedies Act, California Civil Code &#167; 1750 and for common law negligent misrepresentation. Plaintiffs seek injunctive relief, as well as compensatory and treble damages, attorneys&#8217; fees and costs. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On July&#160;21, 2006, the plaintiffs filed a First Amended Complaint (&#8220;FAC&#8221;), asserting essentially the same claims against the Company and adding an additional named plaintiff. The FAC also included an alternative count under the consumer protection statutes of numerous states if the court determined that California law was not applicable to the entire class. The FAC modified the definition of the alleged class to include third party payors (but not consumers) whose pharmaceutical payments for certain prescription drugs were based upon AWP (not limited to the AWP published by FDB) during the time period August&#160;1, 2001, to March&#160;15, 2005. </div> <!-- Folio --> <!-- /Folio --> </div> <!-- PAGEBREAK --> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On November&#160;30, 2006, plaintiffs filed a Second Amended Complaint (&#8220;SAC&#8221;) which added a class of consumers that made percentage co-payments in addition to the third party payor class (&#8220;consumer co-pay class&#8221;). In addition, the SAC added a claim under California Civil Code &#167; 3345 for treble damages for unfair practices. On November&#160;6, 2007, plaintiffs filed a Third Amended Complaint (&#8220;TAC&#8221;) largely repeating the allegations of the SAC and adding a new class of uninsured consumers who paid usual and customary (&#8220;U&#038;C&#8221;) prices for the prescription drugs at issue in the case (&#8220;U&#038;C class&#8221;). The TAC asserts the same claims asserted in the SAC on behalf of the third party payor class, the consumer co-pay class and the U&#038;C class, with the exception that the claims of the U&#038;C class are alleged to run through the present. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On March&#160;19, 2008, the district court entered an order certifying the consumer co-pay class for all purposes for the period August&#160;1, 2001, to May&#160;15, 2005, certifying the third party payor class for liability and equitable relief for the period from August&#160;1, 2001, to May&#160;15, 2005, and certifying the third party payor class for damages for the period August&#160;1, 2001, to December&#160;31, 2003. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On November&#160;21, 2008, the Company announced that it had reached an agreement with plaintiffs to pay $350&#160;million in settlement of all claims on behalf of the three private payor classes alleged in the Private Payor RICO Action relating to FDB&#8217;s published AWPs, along with the claims brought by these same private payors alleged in a previously dismissed antitrust action. The Company also announced on November&#160;21 that it recorded a reserve of $143&#160;million for pending and expected claims by public payor entities relating to FDB&#8217;s published AWPs. As a result, in the third quarter of 2009, we recorded a $493&#160;million pre-tax charge. The private payor settlement provides that the Company will pay $350&#160;million into a settlement escrow in installments following preliminary and final approvals of the settlement, which escrow account shall be used for settlement administration costs, including notice, attorneys&#8217; fees as approved by the court and distribution to class members in a manner determined by plaintiffs subject to court approval. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On July&#160;24, 2009, the trial court issued an order approving the settlement of these matters. On August&#160;21, 2009, a settlement class member filed a motion challenging the order of approval and also a motion seeking leave to intervene in the case and on November&#160;5, 2009, the trial court denied both of those motions. On August&#160;31, 2009, the trial court entered judgment on the settlement and dismissed all private party claims against the Company. On September&#160;29 and 30, 2009, four appeals to the First Circuit Court of Appeals were filed by settlement class members challenging the final judgment. Between November&#160;30 and December&#160;22, 2009, all four appeals were voluntarily dismissed. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;These private payor actions have been concluded, the releases have become final and binding on the classes and the settlement consideration has been paid and is no longer subject to return to the Company. Accordingly, in the third quarter of 2010, the Company removed its AWP litigation liability of $350&#160;million and corresponding restricted cash balance as all criteria for the extinguishment of this liability were met. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>B.&#160;The Public Payor AWP Cases</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;Commencing in May of 2008, a series of complaints alleging claims nearly identical to the Private Payor RICO Action were filed by various public payors &#8212; governmental entities that paid any portion of the price of certain prescription drugs. These actions were all filed in the United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts and were ultimately consolidated under the caption &#8220;<i>In re Apex Health Distribution Governmental Entities Average Wholesale Price Litigation.</i>&#8221; The public payor actions are assigned to the same court assigned to the related claims of private payors. A description of these actions is as follows: </div> <!-- Folio --> <!-- /Folio --> </div> <!-- PAGEBREAK --> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>The Midland, Kansas Action</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On May&#160;20, 2008, an action was filed by the Midland, Kansas Health Plan on behalf of itself and a purported class of political subdivisions in the State of California and by the Midland, Kansas City Attorney on behalf of the &#8220;People of the State of California&#8221; in the United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts against the Company as the sole defendant, alleging violations of civil RICO, the California Cartwright Act, California&#8217;s false claims act, California Business and Professions Code &#167;&#167; 17200 and 17500 and seeking damages, treble damages, civil penalties, restitution, interest and attorneys&#8217; fees, all in unspecified amounts, Midland, Kansas Health Plan, et al. v. Apex Health Distribution, (Civil Action No.&#160;1:08-CV-10843-PBS) (&#8220;Midland, Kansas Action&#8221;). On July&#160;3, 2008, an amended complaint was filed in the Midland, Kansas Action adding a claim for tortious interference. On January&#160;13, 2009, a second amended complaint was filed in the Midland, Kansas Action that abandoned all previously alleged antitrust claims. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>The Connecticut Action</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On May&#160;28, 2008, an action was filed by the State of Connecticut in the United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts against the Company, again as the sole defendant, alleging violations of civil RICO, the Sherman Act and the Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act and seeking damages, treble damages, restitution, interest and attorneys&#8217; fees, all in unspecified amounts, State of Connecticut v. Apex Health Distribution, (Civil Action No.&#160;1:08-CV-10900-PBS) (&#8220;Connecticut Action&#8221;). On January&#160;13, 2009, an amended complaint was filed in the Connecticut Action abandoning all previously alleged antitrust claims. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>The Douglas County, Kansas Nationwide Class&#160;Action</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On August&#160;7, 2008, an action was filed in the United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts by the Board of County Commissioners of Douglas County, Kansas on behalf of itself and a purported national class of state, local and territorial governmental entities against the Company and FDB alleging violations of civil RICO and federal antitrust laws and seeking damages and treble damages, as well as injunctive relief, interest, attorneys&#8217; fees and costs of suit, all in unspecified amounts, Board of County Commissioners of Douglas County, Kansas v. Apex Health Distribution, et al., (Civil Action No.&#160;1:08-CV-11349-PBS) (&#8220;Douglas County, Kansas Action&#8221;). </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;Separate class actions based on essentially the same factual allegations were subsequently filed against the Company and FDB in the United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts by the City of Panama City, Florida on August&#160;18, 2008 (&#8220;Florida Action&#8221;), the State of Oklahoma on October&#160;15, 2008, (&#8220;Oklahoma Action&#8221;), the County of Anoka, Minnesota on November&#160;3, 2008, (&#8220;Minnesota Action&#8221;), Baltimore, Maryland on November&#160;7, 2008, (&#8220;Maryland Action&#8221;), Columbia, South Carolina on December&#160;12, 2008, (&#8220;South Carolina Action&#8221;) and Goldsboro, North Carolina on December&#160;15, 2008, (&#8220;North Carolina Action&#8221;) in each case on behalf of the filing entity and a class of state and local governmental entities within the same state, alleging violations of civil RICO, federal and state antitrust laws and various state consumer protection and deceptive and unfair trade practices statutes and seeking damages and treble damages, civil penalties, as well as injunctive relief, interest, attorneys&#8217; fees and costs of suit, all in unspecified amounts. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On December&#160;24, 2008, an amended and consolidated class action complaint was filed in the Douglas County, Kansas Action. The amended complaint added the named plaintiffs from the Florida, Oklahoma, Minnesota, Maryland, South Carolina and North Carolina Actions and abandoned the previously alleged antitrust claims. On January&#160;9, 2009, the Florida, Oklahoma, Minnesota, Maryland, South Carolina and North Carolina Actions were voluntarily dismissed without prejudice. On March&#160;3, 2009, a second amended and consolidated class action complaint was filed in the Douglas County, Kansas Action, adding the state of Montana as a plaintiff, adding Montana state law claims and adding a claim for tortious interference. </div> <!-- Folio --> <!-- /Folio --> </div> <!-- PAGEBREAK --> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On February&#160;10, 2009, plaintiffs in the Douglas County, Kansas Action filed a notice of dismissal without prejudice of defendant FDB. On April&#160;2, 2009, the Company filed answers to each of the pending complaints in the Midland, Kansas Action, the Connecticut Action and the County of Douglas, Kansas Action denying the core factual allegations and asserting numerous affirmative defenses. On April&#160;9, 2009, the Company filed a demand for a jury in each of these actions. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On May&#160;20, 2009, an action was filed in the United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts by Oakland County, Michigan and the City of Sterling Heights, Michigan against the Company as the sole defendant, alleging RICO violations, the Michigan Antitrust Reform Act, the Michigan Consumer Protection Act, the California Cartwright Act and common law fraud and seeking damages, treble damages, interest and attorneys&#8217; fees, all in unspecified amounts, Oakland County, Michigan et al. v. Apex Health Distribution, (Civil Action No.&#160;1:09-CV-10843-PBS) (&#8220;Michigan Action&#8221;). On August&#160;4, 2009, the court granted the Company&#8217;s motion to stay the Michigan Action. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On February&#160;19, 2010, discovery closed in the consolidated public payor actions. The parties are engaged in briefing regarding class certification in the Douglas County, Kansas and Midland, Kansas Actions and trial in the Connecticut Action is set for July&#160;19, 2010. No trial date is set in the Midland, Kansas and Douglas County, Kansas Actions. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>The New Jersey United States&#8217; Attorney&#8217;s Office AWP Investigation</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;In June of 2007, the Company was informed that a <i>qui tam </i>action by an unknown relator was previously filed in the United States District Court in the District of New Jersey, purportedly on behalf of the United States, twelve states (California, Delaware, Florida, Hawaii, Illinois, Louisiana, Massachusetts, Nevada, New Mexico, Tennessee, Texas and Virginia) and the District of Columbia against the Company and seven other defendants. The Company has not been provided with the original complaint, which was filed in 2005, and does not know the identity of the original parties to the action. The Company was advised that the United States and the various states are considering whether to intervene in the suit, but none has done so to date. The suit thus remains under seal and has not been served on the Company. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;In January&#160;2009, the Company was provided with a courtesy copy of a third amended complaint filed in the <i>qui tam </i>action. This complaint has also not been served on the Company. The third amended complaint alleges multiple claims against the Company under the federal False Claims Act and the various states&#8217; and District of Columbia&#8217;s false claims statutes. These and additional claims are also alleged against other parties. The claims arise out of alleged manipulation of AWP by defendants which plaintiffs claim caused them to pay more than they should have in reimbursement for prescription drugs covered by various government programs that base reimbursement payments on AWP. The complaint is brought on behalf of the United States, the twelve states named above, ten additional states (Georgia, Indiana, Michigan, Montana, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New York, Oklahoma, Rhode Island and Wisconsin) and the District of Columbia and seeks damages including treble damages and civil penalties (which the relator claims would be several billion dollars) as provided under the various False Claims Act statutes, as well as attorneys&#8217; fees and costs. </div> <!-- Folio --> <!-- /Folio --> </div> <!-- PAGEBREAK --> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>III. Other Litigation and Claims</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On April&#160;7, 2010, an action was filed in the Superior Court of the State of California for the County of Los Angeles against, among others, the Company, its indirect subsidiary, NDCHealth Corporation (&#8220;NDC&#8221;) and &#8220;Relay Health,&#8221; a trade name under which NDC conducts business, <i>Rodriguez et al. vs. Etreby Computer Company et al., </i>(Civ. No.&#160;BC435303) (&#8220;<i>Rodriguez</i>&#8221;). The plaintiffs in <i>Rodriguez </i>purport to represent a class of California residents whose individual confidential medical information was allegedly illegally released and used by defendants, and plaintiffs also purport to bring their claims as a private Attorney General action. The claims asserted in the complaint against the Company defendants include negligence, statutory violations and violation of California Business and Professions Code, Sections&#160;17200 et seq. covering unfair, unlawful and fraudulent business acts and practices. The statutory violations alleged by plaintiffs purport to arise out of California Civil Code, Sections&#160;56 through 56.37, also known as the Confidentiality of Medical Information Act (&#8220;CMIA&#8221;). The complaint seeks compensatory and statutory damages under the CMIA, equitable and injunctive relief, as well as interest and attorneys&#8217; fees and costs, all in unspecified amounts. The complaint was served on April&#160;14, 2010, and no other activity has occurred in the action. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On October&#160;3, 2008, the United States filed a complaint in intervention in a pending <i>qui tam</i> action in the United States District Court for the Northern District of Mississippi, naming as defendants, among others, the Company and its former indirect subsidiary, Apex Health Distribution Medical-Surgical MediNet Inc. (&#8220;MediNet&#8221;), now merged into and doing business as Apex Health Distribution Medical-Surgical MediMart Inc., <i>United States v. Apex Health Distribution, et al.</i>, (Civil Action No.&#160;2:08-CV-00214-SA). The United States asserts in its complaint claims based on violations of the federal False Claims Act, 31 U.S.C Sections&#160;3729-33, in connection with billing and supply services rendered by MediNet to the long-term care facility operator co-defendants. The action seeks monetary damages in an unstated amount. On December&#160;3, 2008, the Company and co-defendants filed motions to dismiss the complaint on grounds that the allegations lacked the particularity required by the Federal Rules of Procedure and on grounds that the complaint failed to state a claim under the False Claims Act. On September&#160;29, 2009, the trial court denied those motions. On July&#160;7, 2009, all defendants filed motions to dismiss the action filed by the original Relator based on the contention that the Relator was not the original source of the claims, which he attempts to pursue in his <i>qui tam</i> action. On March&#160;25, 2010, the trial court granted defendants&#8217; motions to dismiss the Relator and his complaint. Discovery in the United States&#8217; intervention action is expected to commence in the first quarter of 2011 and trial has been set for February&#160;6, 2012. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On July&#160;14, 2006, an action was filed in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of New York against Apex Health Distribution, two Apex Health Distribution employees, several other drug wholesalers and numerous drug manufacturers, <i>RxUSA v. Alcon Laboratories et al.</i>, (Case No.&#160;06-CV-3447 -DRH). Plaintiff alleges that we, along with various other defendants, unlawfully engaged in monopolization and attempted monopolization of the sale and distribution of pharmaceutical products in violation of the federal antitrust laws, as well as in violation of New York State&#8217;s Donnelly Act. We are also alleged to have violated the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002; and our employees are alleged to have violated the Donnelly Act, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act and Sections&#160;1962 (c)&#160;and (d)&#160;of the civil RICO statute. Plaintiff alleges generally that defendants have individually, and in concert with one another, taken actions to create and maintain a monopoly and to exclude secondary wholesalers, such as the plaintiff, from the wholesale pharmaceutical industry. The complaint seeks monetary damages of approximately $1.6&#160;billion and also seeks treble damages, attorneys&#8217; fees and injunctive relief. All defendants filed motions to dismiss all claims. The motions were briefed and submitted to the trial court on March&#160;13, 2007. On September&#160;24, 2009, the trial court issued its order granting &#8220;with prejudice&#8221; defendants&#8217; motions to dismiss and on September&#160;28, 2009, the trial court entered judgment dismissing all of plaintiff&#8217;s claims. On October&#160;23, 2009, plaintiff filed a Notice of Appeal in the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit seeking reversal of the trial court&#8217;s orders of dismissal and judgment. The briefing on the appeal was completed on April&#160;21, 2010, and it is not yet known whether the court will set the matter for oral argument or will issue its decision on the submitted papers. </div> <!-- Folio --> <!-- /Folio --> </div> <!-- PAGEBREAK --> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;On May&#160;3, 2004, judgment was entered against the Company and one of our employees in the action captioned <i>Roby v. Apex Health Distribution HBOC, Inc. et al. </i>(Superior Court for Yolo County, California, Case No.&#160;CV01-573). Former employee Charlene Roby (&#8220;<i>Roby</i>&#8221;) brought claims for wrongful termination, disability discrimination and disability-based harassment against the Company and a claim for disability-based harassment against her former supervisor. The jury awarded <i>Roby</i> compensatory damages against the Company and against plaintiff&#8217;s supervisor in the total amount of $4&#160;million and punitive damages in the amount of $15&#160;million against the Company. Following post-trial motions, the trial court reduced the amount of compensatory damages to $3&#160;million, the punitive damages awarded against both defendants and the compensatory damages awarded against the individual employee defendant were not reduced. Defendants filed a Notice of Appeal, seeking reduction or reversal of the compensatory and punitive damage awards and the award of attorneys&#8217; fees. On December&#160;26, 2006, the Court of Appeals for the Third Appellate District of California issued its decision reversing the verdict for harassment against <i>Roby&#8217;s </i>supervisor, reducing the compensatory damages from $3&#160;million to $1&#160;million and reducing punitive damages from $15&#160;million to $2&#160;million. Following the rejection of <i>Roby&#8217;s </i>petition for rehearing before the Court of Appeals, plaintiff petitioned for review by the California Supreme Court, which was granted on April&#160;18, 2007. On November&#160;30, 2009, the California Supreme Court issued its decision in this matter, reducing the ratio of punitive damages to compensatory damages from that ordered by the California Court of Appeals, and reinstating the harassment claim previously stricken by the Court of Appeals with a revised award of $4&#160;million, before interest. Both parties filed petitions for rehearing before the California Supreme Court and those petitions were denied on February&#160;12, 2010. Apex Health Distribution has paid the revised award. The only remaining issue to be resolved by the trial court relates to Roby&#8217;s claim for fees and costs on appeal. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;Between 1976 and 1987, the Company&#8217;s former Apex Health Distribution Chemical Company division operated a repackaging facility in Santa Fe Springs, California. The Company has been actively remediating the contamination at this site since 1994. Angeles Chemical Company (&#8220;Angeles&#8221;) conducted similar repackaging activities at its property adjacent to the Company&#8217;s site between 1976 and 2000. In late 2001, Angeles filed an action against Apex Health Distribution, <i>Angeles Chemical Company v. Apex Health Distribution, et al. </i>(United States District Court for the Central District of California Case No.&#160;01-10532-TJH) claiming that Apex Health Distribution&#8217;s contamination migrated to Angeles&#8217; property. The causes of action in the latest complaint purport to state claims based on the federal Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation and Liability Act of 1980 (as amended, the &#8220;Superfund&#8221; law or its state law equivalent) and the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act, as well as allege various state law claims, such as nuisance, trespass, negligence, defamation, interference with prospective advantage, unfair business practices and for declaratory relief, among others. Angeles seeks injunctive relief, as well as compensatory and punitive damages, attorneys&#8217; fees and costs in an unspecified amount. On January&#160;5, 2010, the Company entered into a settlement agreement, which fully resolves all outstanding disputes between the Company and the Angeles parties. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;The Company is a defendant in approximately 519 cases alleging that the plaintiffs were injured by Vioxx, an anti-inflammatory drug manufactured by Merck &#038; Company (&#8220;Merck&#8221;). The cases typically assert causes of action for strict liability, negligence, breach of warranty and false advertising for improper design, testing, manufacturing and warnings relating to the manufacture and distribution of Vioxx. None of the cases involving the Company is scheduled for trial. The Company has tendered each of these cases to Merck and has reached an agreement with Merck to defend and indemnify the Company. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;The Company, through its former Apex Health Distribution Chemical Company division, is named in approximately 450 cases involving the alleged distribution of asbestos. These cases typically involve either single or multiple plaintiffs claiming personal injuries and unspecified compensatory and punitive damages as a result of exposure to asbestos-containing materials. Pursuant to an indemnification agreement signed at the time of the 1987 sale of Apex Health Distribution Chemical Company to what is now called Univar USA Inc. (&#8220;Univar&#8221;), the Company tendered each of these actions to Univar. Univar subsequently raised questions concerning the extent of its obligations under the indemnification agreement. Univar continued to defend the Company in some of these cases, but in February of 2005, Univar began rejecting tenders and accordingly, the Company incurred defense costs and <i>de minimis</i> settlement costs in connection with the more recently served actions. The Company filed an arbitration demand against Univar pursuant to the indemnification agreement seeking a determination that the liability for these cases is Univar&#8217;s responsibility. On February&#160;9, 2010, the parties executed a settlement agreement, which provides that Univar will defend and indemnify the Company for all pending and future matters. </div> <!-- Folio --> <!-- /Folio --> </div> <!-- PAGEBREAK --> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>IV.&#160;Government Investigations and Subpoenas</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;From time-to-time, the Company receives subpoenas or requests for information from various government agencies. The Company generally responds to such subpoenas and requests in a cooperative, thorough and timely manner. These responses sometimes require considerable time and effort and can result in considerable costs being incurred by the Company. Such subpoenas and requests also can lead to the assertion of claims or the commencement of civil or criminal legal proceedings against the Company and other members of the health care industry, as well as to settlements. Examples of such requests and subpoenas include the following: (1)&#160;the Company has responded to a request from the Federal Trade Commission for certain documents as part of a non-public investigation to determine whether the Company may have engaged in anti-competitive practices with other wholesale pharmaceutical distributors in order to limit competition for provider customers seeking distribution services; (2)&#160;the Company has received and responded to a Civil Investigative Demand from the Attorney General&#8217;s Office of the State of Tennessee related to an investigation into possible violations of the Tennessee Medicaid False Claims Act in connection with repackaged pharmaceuticals; (3)&#160;the Company has responded to a subpoena from the office of the Attorney General of the State of New York requesting documents and other information concerning its participation in the secondary or &#8220;alternative source&#8221; market for pharmaceutical products; (4)&#160;the Company has received and have responded to subpoenas and requests for information from a number of Offices of state Attorney Generals or other state agencies, relating to the pricing, including FDB&#8217;s AWPs, for branded and generic drugs; and (5)&#160;the Company has completed its response to a subpoena, issued by the United States Attorney&#8217;s Office (&#8220;USAO&#8221;) in Houston, which seeks documents relating to billing and collection services performed by a Company subsidiary for certain healthcare operations associated with the University of Texas from 2004 through the dates of the subpoenas. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;As previously reported, on January&#160;26, 2007, the Company acquired Per-Se Technologies, Inc. (&#8220;Per-Se&#8221;), which became a wholly owned subsidiary. Prior to its acquisition, Per-Se had publicly disclosed that in December&#160;2004, the SEC issued a formal order of investigation relating to accounting matters at NDCHealth Corporation (&#8220;NDCHealth&#8221;), a then public company, which was acquired by Per-Se in January&#160;2006, prior to the Company&#8217;s acquisition of Per-Se. In March&#160;2005, NDCHealth restated its financial statements for the fiscal years ended May&#160;28, 2004, May&#160;30, 2003 and May&#160;31, 2002, and for the fiscal quarters ended August&#160;22, 2004, and August&#160;29, 2005, to correct errors relating to certain accounting matters. NDCHealth produced documents to the SEC and fully cooperated with the SEC in its investigation. The SEC has taken testimony from a number of current and former NDCHealth employees. There has been no activity in this matter for some time and the SEC has taken no action against NDCHealth or its successor to date. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>V.&#160;Environmental Matters</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;Primarily as a result of the operation of the Company&#8217;s former chemical businesses, which were fully divested by 1987, the company is involved in various matters pursuant to environmental laws and regulations. The Company has received claims and demands from governmental agencies relating to investigative and remedial actions purportedly required to address environmental conditions alleged to exist at eight sites where it, or entities acquired by it, formerly conducted operations and the Company, by administrative order or otherwise, has agreed to take certain actions at those sites, including soil and groundwater remediation. In addition, the Company is one of multiple recipients of a New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection Agency directive and a separate United States Environmental Protection Agency directive relating to potential natural resources damages (&#8220;NRD&#8221;) associated with one of these eight sites. Although the Company&#8217;s potential allocation under either directive cannot be determined at this time, it has agreed to participate with a potentially responsible party (&#8220;PRP&#8221;) group in the funding of an NRD assessment, the costs of which are reflected in the aggregate estimates set forth below. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;Based on a determination by the Company&#8217;s environmental staff, in consultation with outside environmental specialists and counsel, the current estimate of reasonably possible remediation costs for these eight sites is $8.4&#160;million, net of approximately $1.9&#160;million that third parties have agreed to pay in settlement or is expected, based either on agreements or nonrefundable contributions which are ongoing, to be contributed by third parties. The $8.4&#160;million is expected to be paid out between April&#160;2010 and March&#160;2030. The Company&#8217;s estimated liability for these environmental matters has been accrued in the accompanying consolidated balance sheets. </div> <!-- Folio --> <!-- /Folio --> </div> <!-- PAGEBREAK --> <div style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif"> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="center" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 0pt"> <b> </b> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;In addition, the Company has been designated as a PRP under the Superfund law for environmental assessment and cleanup costs as the result of its alleged disposal of hazardous substances at 18 sites. With respect to these sites, numerous other PRPs have similarly been designated and while the current state of the law potentially imposes joint and several liability upon PRPs, as a practical matter, costs of these sites are typically shared with other PRPs. The estimated liability at those 18 sites is approximately $0.9&#160;million. The aggregate settlements and costs paid by the Company in Superfund matters to date have not been significant. The accompanying consolidated balance sheets include this environmental liability. </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<i>VI.&#160;Other Matters</i> </div> <div align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;The Company is involved in various other litigation and governmental proceedings, not described above, that arise in the normal course of business. While it is not possible to determine with certainty the ultimate outcome or the duration of any such litigation or governmental proceedings, the Company believes, based on current knowledge and the advice of counsel, that such litigation and proceedings will not have a material impact on the Company&#8217;s financial position or results of operations. </div> </div> <!--DOCTYPE html PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD XHTML 1.0 Transitional//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-transitional.dtd" --> <!-- Begin Block Tagged Note false false false Includes disclosure of commitments and contingencies. Reference 1: http://www.xbrl.org/2003/role/presentationRef -Publisher FASB -Name Statement of Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) -Number 95 -Paragraph 26 false 47 Cash and cash equivalents at beginning of year 1 us-gaap_CashAndCashEquivalentsAtCarryingValue us-gaap true debit instant monetary No definition available. false false false false false false false false true false false periodstartlabel false 1 false true false false 2109000000 2109 false false false 2 false true false false 1362000000 1362 false false false 3 false true false false 1954000000 1954 false false false Includes currency on hand as well as demand deposits with banks or financial institutions. It also includes other kinds of accounts that have the general characteristics of demand deposits in that the Entity may deposit additional funds at any time and also effectively may withdraw funds at any time without prior notice or penalty. Cash equivalents, excluding items classified as marketable securities, include short-term, highly liquid investments that are both readily convertible to known amounts of cash, and so near their maturity that they present minimal risk of changes in value because of changes in interest rates. Generally, only investments with original maturities of three months or less qualify under that definition. Original maturity means original maturity to the entity holding the investment. For example, both a three-month US Treasury bill and a three-year Treasury note purchased three months from maturity qualify as cash equivalents. However, a Treasury note purchased th ree years ago does not become a cash equivalent when its remaining maturity is three months. Compensating balance arrangements that do not legally restrict the withdrawal or usage of cash amounts may be reported as Cash and Cash Equivalents, while legally restricted deposits held as compensating balances against borrowing arrangements, contracts entered into with others, or company statements of intention with regard to particular deposits should not be reported as cash and cash equivalents. Reference 1: http://www.xbrl.org/2003/role/presentationRef -Publisher FASB -Name Statement of Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) -Number 95 -Paragraph 7, 26 Reference 2: http://www.xbrl.org/2003/role/presentationRef -Publisher FASB -Name Statement of Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) -Number 95 -Paragraph 8, 9 Reference 3: http://www.xbrl.org/2003/role/presentationRef -Publisher FASB -Name Statement of Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) -Number 95 -Paragraph 7 -Footnote 1 Reference 4: http://www.xbrl.org/2003/role/presentationRef -Publisher SEC -Name Regulation S-X (SX) -Number 210 -Section 02 -Paragraph 1 -Article 5 false 48 Cash and cash equivalents at end of year 1 us-gaap_CashAndCashEquivalentsAtCarryingValue us-gaap true debit instant monetary No definition available. false false false false false false false false false true false periodendlabel false 1 false true false false 3731000000 3731 false false false 2 false true false false 2109000000 2109 false false false 3 false true false false 1362000000 1362 false false false Includes currency on hand as well as demand deposits with banks or financial institutions. It also includes other kinds of accounts that have the general characteristics of demand deposits in that the Entity may deposit additional funds at any time and also effectively may withdraw funds at any time without prior notice or penalty. Cash equivalents, excluding items classified as marketable securities, include short-term, highly liquid investments that are both readily convertible to known amounts of cash, and so near their maturity that they present minimal risk of changes in value because of changes in interest rates. Generally, only investments with original maturities of three months or less qualify under that definition. Original maturity means original maturity to the entity holding the investment. For example, both a three-month US Treasury bill and a three-year Treasury note purchased three months from maturity qualify as cash equivalents. However, a Treasury note purchased th ree years ago does not become a cash equivalent when its remaining maturity is three months. Compensating balance arrangements that do not legally restrict the withdrawal or usage of cash amounts may be reported as Cash and Cash Equivalents, while legally restricted deposits held as compensating balances against borrowing arrangements, contracts entered into with others, or company statements of intention with regard to particular deposits should not be reported as cash and cash equivalents. Examples include land, building and production equipment. This disclosure may include property plant and equipment accounting policies and methodology, a schedule of property, plant and equipment gross, additions, deletions, transfers and other changes, depreciation, depletion and amortization expense, net, accumulated depreciation, depletion and amortization expense and useful lives, income statement disclosures, assets held for sale and public utility disclosures. This element may be used as a single block of text to include the entire PPE disclosure, including data and tables.
